title: Zero-Field Thermal Hall Effect in Insulator
authors: Zhe Cui, Haoran Fan, Wenjiang Zhou, Xianghong Jin, Yuchen Gu, Da Ma, Cong Xiao, Hua Jiang, Xincheng Xie, Bai Song, Yuan Li, Xi Lin
abstract: Fourier's law dictates that heat flow is usually parallel to the applied temperature gradient. However, under a high magnetic field, heat flow carried by both electrons in conductors and phonons in insulators can be deflected, a phenomenon known as thermal Hall effect. Intriguingly, we observe at zero field a spontaneous thermal Hall effect in an antiferromagnetic insulator. Despite a vanishingly small uncompensated magnetization, the magnitude of this effect is surprisingly large, comparable to typical responses induced by several teslas of external field. This zero-field behavior indicates that charge-neutral heat carriers can be governed by an intrinsic effective field arising from the unique spin arrangement. Our discovery challenges the centuries-old preconception of heat conduction and open up new avenues for exploring non-trivial topological responses in quantum materials.
summary: This paper reports the discovery of a spontaneous thermal Hall effect in the antiferromagnetic insulator Na2Co2TeO6 at zero external magnetic field. The effect is driven by an intrinsic effective field arising from a unique non-coplanar spin texture. This finding challenges traditional views on heat conduction and provides a new way to explore topological responses in quantum materials.
main_problem: The study aims to understand the origin of a spontaneous thermal Hall effect (STHE) in an antiferromagnetic insulator in the absence of an external magnetic field.
main_result: The researchers discovered a large zero-field thermal Hall effect in Na2Co2TeO6, which is attributed to an intrinsic effective magnetic field generated by a non-coplanar spin texture (vortices and magnetization) rather than a net magnetic moment.
method: Thermal Hall conductivity measurements were performed on single-crystal Na2Co2TeO6 using a cryostat, applying a longitudinal heat flow and detecting transverse temperature gradients with high-resolution thermocouples.
model_or_system: The physical system is the honeycomb oxide Na2Co2TeO6, an antiferromagnetic insulator characterized by in-plane vortices and nearly compensated out-of-plane magnetic moments.
key_observables: Transverse thermal conductivity (kappa_xy), longitudinal thermal conductivity (kappa_xx), thermal Hall angle (kappa_xy/kappa_xx), and magnetization (m).
important_parameters: Neeel temperature (TN approx 27 K), compensation temperature (T*), and effective magnetic field (B_eff).
main_assumptions: The sample is assumed to be in a single antiferromagnetic domain achieved through magnetic field training.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the experimental setup, sample training procedure, and raw temperature data; Fig 2 illustrates the spin arrangement and the temperature dependence of the zero-field thermal Hall signal; Fig 3 compares the thermal Hall response under effective and external magnetic fields.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the phenomenon of the thermal Hall effect, describes the experimental setup and sample preparation, presents the discovery of the zero-field effect and its temperature dependence, proposes a mechanism based on an effective field from spin textures, and compares the results to external field-induced effects.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 | Measurement of thermal Hall effect in Na2Co2TeO6. a, Schematic of the experimental setup. Heat flow is applied along the a-axis via a heater. The longitudinal and transverse temperature differences (ΔTxx and ΔTxy) are measured by two sets of thermocouples, with the same sign convention of electrical Hall effect. An external magnetic field can be applied along the c-axis. b, Sample preparation procedure. First, a weak trained field +Btrain is applied with the sample temperature above its Néel temperature TN ≈ 27 K. The sample is then cooled down to Tbase. Finally, the field is removed, leaving Na2Co2TeO6 in an antiferromagnetic ground state with single domain (concerning magnetic...

Fig. 2 | Thermal Hall effect at zero magnetic field. a, Schematic illustration of the spin arrangement and heat flow. For a given training process, the nearly compensated out-of-plane magnetization m and the in-plane vortices v act together to the deflection of heat flow. b, Temperature dependence of -κxy at zero magnetic field. The solid line is a guide to the eyes. The error bars are obtained from the measurement noises, as shown the grey bar in Fig. 1c. Inset shows both κₓₓ and -κxy in units of W m-1 K-1. Remarkably, reversal point of -κxy coincides with the compensation point T* of m, while above TN there is no detectable -κxy, including the temperature Tp of maximum κₓₓ.

Fig. 3 | Thermal Hall effect under effective magnetic field Beff and external magnetic field Bext. a, κxy/T as a function of Beff at Bext = 0 T , where Beff ∝𝑚∙𝑣2 . Top-left inset: Comparison of Beff and m with κxy/T. Bottom-right inset: Illustration of heat flow deflected by Beff. b, κxy/T as a function of Bext at different temperatures above TN. The dashed lines are linear fittings. Top-right inset: κxx under Bext = 9 T, -κxy under Bext = 9 T, 6 T, and 3 T. For -κxy, two distinct regions are highlighted with blue and purple shades, which are separated by T = TN.
